literal: self; reflexively, that which one refers to as 'I'; from a root meaning 'breath' (cf. Greek *atmos*) but used in classical Sanskrit purely for the self
invariant_definition: The innermost reality of the conscious being, that which is referred to by 'I' when 'I' is used in its strictest sense (not the body, not the *manas*, not the *ahaṅkāra*). Every Vedāntic school agrees that *ātman* is *cit* (consciousness), is the witness of the three states (waking, dreaming, deep sleep), and is what survives across embodiments. Schools diverge on whether *ātman* is numerically identical with *brahman* (Advaita), is a mode of *brahman* really distinct from it (Viśiṣṭādvaita), or is an eternally distinct dependent reality (Dvaita). Note: *ātman* and *jīva* are often used interchangeably in school usage but technically *jīva* foregrounds the embodied locus while *ātman* foregrounds the inmost essence.

per_school.definition: *Ātman* is numerically identical with *brahman*. The *mahāvākya tat tvam asi* (Chāndogya 6.8.7) and *ayam ātmā brahma* (Māṇḍūkya 2) are read as identity-statements (*akhaṇḍārtha*), not as relational predications. The apparent multiplicity of *ātmans* is a function of *upādhi*-superimposition; on liberation the *upādhis* fall away and there is realized to have been only one *ātman* throughout.

*Ātman* is an eternally real, atomic (*aṇu*), self-luminous individual conscious substance that is a real mode (*prakāra*) of *brahman*. The unity asserted in *tat tvam asi* is *sāmānādhikaraṇya* (co-reference) under *aprthak-siddhi*: *tat* (Brahman as soul) and *tvam* (the embodied self) refer to the same reality only because the self is Brahman's body, not because they are numerically identical.
primary_loci: Vedārtha-Saṅgraha §§4–10 (against Advaita reading of tat tvam asi)

per_school.definition: *Ātman* is one of the eternally distinct dependent realities. It is *aṇu* (atomic), *paratantra* (dependent on Viṣṇu), and irreducibly distinct both from *brahman* and from every other *ātman*. *Tat tvam asi* is parsed as *atat tvam asi* ('thou art *not* that') by sandhi-reanalysis, or as predicating qualified similarity (*sādṛśya*), never identity.
primary_loci: Viṣṇu-Tattva-Vinirṇaya §§30–40 (against tat tvam asi as identity)

per_school.definition: *Ātman* is a *vibhinnāṃśa* (separated portion) of Kṛṣṇa — an atomic conscious *aṃśa* of the *taṭasthā-śakti* (marginal power), eternally one with and different from Kṛṣṇa in a way that exceeds logical resolution. The *ātman*'s constitutional position is *dāsya* (servitude) under one of the five *rasas* (śānta, dāsya, sakhya, vātsalya, mādhurya).

translator_note: Translating *ātman* as 'soul' imports Christian-substance and resurrection-of-the-body connotations that distort what every Vedāntin means. Translating it as 'Self' (capitalized) is closer but risks confusing it with the Cartesian-modern individual subject, which is in fact what *ahaṅkāra* names. The Sanskrit *ātman* is the reflexive pronoun used for any being — including animals and inanimate objects in some Upaniṣadic uses — and so is structurally not 'the personal soul' but 'whatever the *I* really is, when correctly identified.'
